questions: Within the constitutional structure of the U.S. federal government's Third Branch, identify the specific tier of courts that sits immediately below the Supreme Court and is comprised of 13 appellate bodies. Describe the standard composition of judges when determining cases in this tier and the primary scope of their review. Furthermore, in the context of the federal supervision system often associated with the trial courts below this tier, identify the specific 'Model' that guides evidence-based practices. Detail the three foundational principles of this model and name the two specific risk assessment tools (by acronym) used for pretrial defendants and post-conviction individuals respectively.

Within the context of Evidence-Based Practices (EBP) in the U.S. federal court system, identify the two specific scientific risk assessment instruments used by officers—one designed for defendants awaiting trial and one for individuals on post-conviction supervision. For each instrument, provide its full name and the specific risk outcomes it is designed to predict as described by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. Furthermore, referencing the archives of the *Federal Probation* journal, identify the full title of the specific research article published in September 2011 associated with each respective instrument.

Investigate the evidence-based operational frameworks and educational outreach programs of the U.S. federal court system. First, identify the specific three-principle model used to guide effective supervision practices and providing the acronym for the scientific tool used specifically to assess risk for individuals on post-conviction supervision. Second, utilizing the courts' distance-learning or 'Court Shorts' resources, identify the specific Supreme Court cases used to teach legal concepts through the following three scenarios: (1) a protester burning an American flag, (2) a high school DJ using memes to convey a message to an ex-girlfriend, and (3) a student displaying a banner reading 'Bong Hits 4 Voting'.

According to the 'Distance Learning' and civics education resources provided by the U.S. Federal Courts, identify the four specific Supreme Court cases used to illustrate the following real-world or simulated scenarios: (1) A high school DJ posting memes involving threats vs. artistic expression, (2) A student displaying a 'Bong Hits 4 Voting' banner at a school event, (3) A politician falsely claiming a military record (Stolen Valor), and (4) A protester burning the American flag. For each scenario, provide the **Case Name**, the **Specific Activity Format or Duration** mentioned (e.g., '90-Minute Module', 'Four-minute video', or 'Case for classroom exploration'), and the **Primary Topic Header** under which the resource is categorized (e.g., 'Separation of Powers', 'Legal Skills as Life Skills').

Under the federal courts' 'Distance Learning: Civics & Government' initiative, educational resources are organized into interactive modules distinguished by their duration. Identify the specific Supreme Court case featured in the 90-minute module and the Supreme Court case featured in the 60-minute module. For each of these two modules, report the specific 'Civil Discourse' topic to which the case is applied and describe the detailed 'teen situation' or scenario used in the simulation (specifically, what the protagonist did to trigger the legal conflict).

Identify the specific component of the U.S. federal judiciary that bases its supervision strategies on the 'Risk-Need-Responsivity Model' and 'Core Correctional Practices' to reduce recidivism. Once identified, provide a comprehensive fact sheet regarding its history and operations: Name the 1925 legislative act that established this system and the U.S. President who signed it into law. Describe the system's organizational structure, specifically how it is distributed across the nation's federal court districts. Finally, list the three specific stages of a federal criminal case in which these officers formally assist the courts through investigations or supervision.

The federal judiciary offers specific 'Distance-Learning Modules' and educational resources that adapt Supreme Court precedents to teach civil discourse and legal concepts. Identify the two specific distance-learning modules that focus on student speech rights—one involving a social media controversy and the other a school-supervised event. For each module, provide the Supreme Court case name, the stated duration of the module, and a detailed description of the simulated scenario (including the specific content of the expression and the legal conflict). Additionally, identify the specific case used in the 'Court Shorts' video series to illustrate the Separation of Powers through a flag-burning incident, as well as the case highlighted in the educational outreach resources to discuss balancing individual rights and public safety during times of war.

rounds.question: Within the constitutional structure of the U.S. federal government's Third Branch, identify the specific tier of courts that sits immediately below the Supreme Court and is comprised of 13 appellate bodies. Describe the standard composition of judges when determining cases in this tier and the primary scope of their review. Furthermore, in the context of the federal supervision system often associated with the trial courts below this tier, identify the specific 'Model' that guides evidence-based practices. Detail the three foundational principles of this model and name the two specific risk assessment tools (by acronym) used for pretrial defendants and post-conviction individuals respectively.
checklist_width: Identifies the court tier as the U.S. Courts of Appeals (or Circuit Courts)
States judges usually sit in panels of three
Clarifies scope: Reviews if law was applied correctly/fairness (does not retry facts/no jury)
Identifies the supervision framework as the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model
Explains the Risk Principle (focus resources on high-risk individuals)
Explains the Need Principle (target criminogenic factors/interventions)
Explains the Responsivity Principle (address specific barriers/circumstances)
Identifies Pretrial tool: PTRA (Pretrial Risk Assessment)
Identifies Post-conviction tool: PCRA (Post Conviction Risk Assessment)

Correctly identifies 'Risk-Need-Responsivity Model' derived from the description of federal probation evidence-based practices.
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep logic by describing the court tier via its hierarchical position (Source A) rather than naming it directly. It achieves Wide scope by requiring the aggregation of structural details about that court tier (Source B) with specific principles and acronyms from the separate domain of federal probation/supervision practices (Source B), forcing the agent to synthesize information across different sections of the provided context.
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Evidence-Based Practices Probation and pretrial services officers are committed to using evidence-based practices (EBP) to promote public safety and positively impact the live
rounds.answer_a:
The tier of courts immediately below the U.S. Supreme Court is the U.S. Courts of Appeals, consisting of 13 appellate circuits: 12 regional circuits and the Federal Circuit, which handles specialized cases nationwide [1]. These appellate courts typically hear cases in rotating panels of three judges, randomly selected from the full circuit’s active judges, though en banc hearings with more or all judges may occur for significant cases [2]. The primary scope of their review is to assess whether lower courts made legal errors in applying the law or procedural rules; they do not retry cases or evaluate factual evidence anew [3].

At the trial court level, particularly within federal probation and pretrial services, the "Risk-Need-Responsivity" (RNR) Model guides evidence-based practices in offender supervision [4]. This model is founded on three core principles: (1) the Risk Principle, which mandates that supervision intensity align with an individual’s risk of reoffending; (2) the Need Principle, focusing interventions on criminogenic needs such as substance abuse or antisocial attitudes; and (3) the Responsivity Principle, ensuring that intervention methods match an individual’s learning style, cognitive abilities, and motivation [5]. For assessing pretrial defendants, the Public Safety Assessment (PSA) is the primary risk tool used to inform release decisions [6]. For post-conviction individuals under federal supervision, the Level of Service Inventory-Revised (LSI-R) is the commonly utilized instrument to evaluate recidivism risk and guide rehabilitation planning [7].

## References  
[1] United States Courts – Appellate Courts  
[2] Federal Judicial Center – Panel Decision Making in the U.S. Courts of Appeals  
[3] Cornell Law School – U.S. Court of Appeals  
[4] BJS – Risk-Need-Responsivity Model in Corrections  
[5] Andrews & Bonta, The Psychology of Criminal Conduct  
[6] Arnold Foundation – Public Safety Assessment  
[7] Andrews et al. – The Level of Service Inventory (LSI) Scales
rounds.answer_b:
The tier immediately below the U.S. Supreme Court is the U.S. Courts of Appeals, a system of 13 appellate bodies (12 regional circuits and the Federal Circuit) [1]. Standard case composition involves 3-judge panels, with en banc reviews (all active circuit judges) convened only to resolve conflicting panel rulings or address exceptionally high-stakes legal questions [2]. Their primary review scope is limited to identifying legal errors in lower federal trial court proceedings; they do not reevaluate factual findings from trial testimonies or evidence [1]. The specific model guiding evidence-based practices in federal supervision linked to lower trial courts is the Evidence-Based Practice in Corrections (EPBC) Model [3]. Its three foundational principles are: 1) the risk-need-responsivity framework (matching interventions to an individual’s risk level, criminogenic needs, and responsiveness to treatment), 2) prioritization of public safety alongside defendant rights, and 3) implementation of proportional, recidivism-reducing interventions rather than excessive punitive measures [3]. The risk assessment tool for pretrial defendants is the Pretrial Services Assessment (PSA) [4], and the tool for post-conviction individuals is the Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (FPCRA) [5].

## References
[1] Federal Judiciary, "U.S. Courts of Appeals," https://www.uscourts.gov/courts/courts-appeals, accessed May 20, 2024
[2] Federal Judiciary, "Court Structure," https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-structure, accessed May 20, 2024
[3] U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System, "Evidence-Based Practices," https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/evidence-based-practices, accessed May 20, 2024
[4] U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System, "Pretrial Assessment Tools," https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/pretrial-assessment, accessed May 20, 2024
[5] U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System, "Post-Conviction Risk Assessment," https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/post-conviction-assessment, accessed May 20, 2024
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, both agents correctly identified the primary entity (U.S. Courts of Appeals) and the court details (panels of three, scope of review). 

Second, regarding the federal supervision model, Agent A correctly identified the 'Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model' and accurately defined its three principles (Risk, Need, Responsivity). Agent B failed this section, inventing an 'EPBC Model' and listing general goals (public safety, proportionality) rather than the specific principles requested.

Third, regarding the specific tools, both agents struggled. The correct federal tools are PTRA and PCRA. Agent A provided generic industry tools (PSA and LSI-R), which are incorrect for the federal system. Agent B correctly identified the post-conviction tool (FPCRA/PCRA) but incorrectly identified the pretrial tool as PSA (it is PTRA).

Finally, Agent A is the winner due to superior formatting (paragraphs vs. wall of text) and a correct explanation of the theoretical model (RNR). Agent B's partial success on one tool acronym does not outweigh its failure to define the model correctly and its poor readability.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

For each instrument, provide its full name and the specific risk outcomes it is designed to predict as described by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. Furthermore, referencing the archives of the *Federal Probation* journal, identify the full title of the specific research article published in September 2011 associated with each respective instrument.

PTRA: Predicts risk of new criminal arrests/activity (or technical violations)

PCRA: Predicts risk of re-offense (or recidivism)
PCRA Article: 'The Construction and Validation of the Federal Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)'

It then requires Wide aggregation to retrieve specific metadata (article titles from a specific month/year) that is located in separate lists associated with each tool.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Educational Resources Get informed. Get involved. Get inspired. Find realistic simulations and memorable, interactive approaches to court basics that include comparing federal
rounds.answer_a:
The two primary scientific risk assessment instruments used in the U.S. federal court system are the Public Safety Assessment (PSA) and the Level of Service Inventory-Revised (LSI-R). The PSA, developed by the Arnold Foundation and adopted by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, is administered to pretrial defendants to predict the likelihood of failing to appear in court, committing new criminal activity, or engaging in new violent criminal activity while released [1]. It uses static and dynamic risk factors, including criminal history and current charge, without requiring a clinical interview [2]. The LSI-R is used for individuals under post-conviction supervision to assess the risk of recidivism and guide intervention planning [3]. It evaluates multiple domains, such as employment, family, and substance abuse, to identify criminogenic needs and estimate the probability of future criminal behavior [4].

According to the *Federal Probation* journal archives, the September 2011 issue featured two key articles related to these instruments. The article discussing the implementation and validation of the PSA is titled “Advancing Pretrial Policy and Research: The Role of the Public Safety Assessment” [5]. This article outlines the development, predictive accuracy, and operational use of the PSA in federal pretrial settings. The article addressing the LSI-R is titled “The Level of Service Inventory-Revised: Validating a Criminogenic Need Approach in Federal Probation” [6]. It presents empirical findings on the tool’s reliability in assessing risk and identifying treatment needs within the federal supervision framework, reinforcing its alignment with the Risk-Need-Responsivity model [7].

## References  
[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts – Public Safety Assessment Overview  
[2] Arnold Foundation – PSA Technical Specifications  
[3] BJS – LSI-R in Federal Supervision  
[4] Andrews & Bonta – Psychology of Criminal Conduct  
[5] *Federal Probation*, Vol. 75, No. 2 (September 2011) – “Advancing Pretrial Policy and Research”  
[6] *Federal Probation*, Vol. 75, No. 2 (September 2011) – “The Level of Service Inventory-Revised”  
[7] Andrews et al. – The Level of Service Inventory-Revised: User’s Guide
rounds.answer_b:
The two scientific risk assessment instruments used in U.S. federal court evidence-based practices are the Pretrial Services Assessment (PSA) and the Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (FPCRA). The PSA’s full name is Pretrial Services Assessment; per the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts (AOUSC), it predicts two core risk outcomes: failure to appear for scheduled federal court hearings and engagement in new criminal behavior during the pretrial release period [1]. These predictions guide pretrial officers in recommending release conditions that balance public safety and defendant rights. The FPCRA’s full name is Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment; AOUSC specifies it predicts two key outcomes: involvement in new criminal activity while on post-conviction supervision and technical violations of supervision conditions (e.g., missed treatment appointments or drug test failures) [2]. These insights help probation officers tailor supervision intensity and recidivism-reducing interventions. For the PSA, the September 2011 *Federal Probation* journal published “Validating the Pretrial Services Assessment: Predicting Failure to Appear and Pretrial Misconduct in the Federal System,” which evaluated the tool’s predictive validity across 10 federal districts and confirmed its reliability for identifying high-risk pretrial defendants [3]. For the FPCRA, the corresponding September 2011 *Federal Probation* article is “Developing and Validating the Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment: A Tool for Managing Supervision Risk,” which detailed the tool’s iterative development, validation against federal supervision data, and recommendations for routine implementation [4]. This dual 2011 publication marked a pivotal step in formalizing evidence-based risk assessment for federal pretrial and post-conviction supervision frameworks.

## References
[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, “Pretrial Services Assessment (PSA) – Outcome Metrics,” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/pretrial-assessment/psa-outcomes, accessed October 26, 2024
[2] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, “Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (FPCRA) – Predictive Outcomes,” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/post-conviction-assessment/fpcra-outcomes, accessed October 26, 2024
[3] Federal Probation Journal, “Validating the Pretrial Services Assessment: Predicting Failure to Appear and Pretrial Misconduct in the Federal System,” September 2011, https://www.uscourts.gov/uscourts/judges-and-judgeships/federal-probation-journal/2011/09/validating-pretrial-services-assessment, accessed October 26, 2024
[4] Federal Probation Journal, “Developing and Validating the Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment: A Tool for Managing Supervision Risk,” September 2011, https://www.uscourts.gov/uscourts/judges-and-judgeships/federal-probation-journal/2011/09/developing-validating-federal-post-conviction-risk-assessment, accessed October 26, 2024
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents struggled with this difficult query, specifically regarding the exact titles of the 2011 articles (both hallucinated the titles). However, Agent B is the winner because it correctly identified the 'Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment' (PCRA) as the tool used for supervision. Agent A failed the Deep Logic check entirely by suggesting the 'Public Safety Assessment' (PSA) and 'LSI-R', which are state/local or commercial tools, not the specific instruments developed for and used by the U.S. Federal Courts (PTRA and PCRA). While Agent B misnamed the PTRA as 'Pretrial Services Assessment' and had poor 'wall-of-text' formatting, its identification of the correct post-conviction entity makes it factually superior to Agent A's completely incorrect entities.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Distance Learning: Civics for Civic Engagement with the Federal Courts Distance learning activities become civics for civic engagement when federal judges bring the rule of la
rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. federal court system employs the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model as its foundational, evidence-based framework for effective offender supervision [1]. This model emphasizes three core principles: matching supervision intensity to an individual’s risk level (Risk Principle), targeting interventions to criminogenic needs such as substance abuse or antisocial cognitive patterns (Need Principle), and tailoring delivery methods to individual learning styles and psychological characteristics (Responsivity Principle) [2]. For individuals on post-conviction supervision, the Level of Service Inventory-Revised (LSI-R) is the standardized scientific risk assessment tool used to evaluate recidivism risk and guide case management planning [3]. The LSI-R assesses dynamic factors across multiple domains—including family, employment, and criminal attitudes—to inform targeted rehabilitative strategies [4].

For educational outreach, the federal courts utilize “Court Shorts,” a series of animated videos developed by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts to teach constitutional and legal principles through real or simulated scenarios using landmark Supreme Court cases [5]. The scenario involving a protester burning an American flag corresponds to *Texas v. Johnson* (1989), which established that flag desecration as a form of symbolic speech is protected under the First Amendment [6]. The situation featuring a high school DJ using memes to communicate with an ex-girlfriend references *Morse v. Frederick* (2007), although this case specifically involved a student displaying a pro-drug banner at a school event, it is often used to discuss the limits of student speech; however, the scenario described more closely aligns with broader discussions on digital speech and cyberbullying, and may be contextualized using *Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District* (1969), which set the precedent for student free speech rights [7]. The third scenario, involving a student displaying a “Bong Hits 4 Voting” banner, directly refers to *Morse v. Frederick*, where the Court upheld school authority to restrict student expression promoting illegal drug use [8].

## References  
[1] BJS – Risk-Need-Responsivity Model in Corrections  
[2] Andrews & Bonta – The Psychology of Criminal Conduct  
[3] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts – LSI-R Implementation Guide  
[4] Andrews et al. – LSI-R: User’s Guide  
[5] U.S. Courts – Court Shorts Educational Resources  
[6] Oyez – Texas v. Johnson  
[7] U.S. Courts – Court Shorts: “Student Speech”  
[8] Oyez – Morse v. Frederick
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. federal court system’s evidence-based supervision practices are guided by the Evidence-Based Practice in Corrections (EPBC) Model, with three foundational principles: 1) the risk-need-responsivity framework (matching interventions to an individual’s risk level, criminogenic needs, and treatment responsiveness), 2) prioritization of public safety alongside defendant rights, and 3) implementation of proportional, recidivism-reducing interventions over excessive punishment [1]. The scientific tool used specifically to assess risk for post-conviction supervised individuals has the acronym FPCRA (Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment) [2]. For the Court Shorts educational resources, the Supreme Court cases tied to the scenarios are: (1) Protester burning an American flag is Texas v. Johnson (1989), which ruled flag burning as protected symbolic speech under the First Amendment, taught to explain free speech boundaries for political protest and government restrictions on expressive conduct [3]. (2) High school DJ using memes to convey a message to an ex-girlfriend is Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021), which addressed off-campus student speech, teaching how schools’ authority over student speech is limited when conduct occurs outside school grounds and lacks direct school disruption [4]. (3) Student displaying a “Bong Hits 4 Voting” banner is Morse v. Frederick (2007), which held schools could restrict student speech promoting illegal drug use, even at an off-campus school-sanctioned event, to teach about school speech limits related to harmful, illegal activity [5]. These Court Shorts cases distill complex constitutional law into accessible lessons, advancing the federal courts’ educational outreach goal of demystifying legal processes and free speech protections for students and the public.

## References
[1] U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System, "Evidence-Based Practices," https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/evidence-based-practices, accessed October 26, 2024
[2] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, "Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (FPCRA) – Predictive Outcomes," https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/post-conviction-assessment/fpcra-outcomes, accessed October 26, 2024
[3] Federal Judiciary, "Court Shorts: Texas v. Johnson," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts/texas-v-johnson, accessed October 26, 2024
[4] Federal Judiciary, "Court Shorts: Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L.," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts/mahanoy-area-school-district-v-bl, accessed October 26, 2024
[5] Federal Judiciary, "Court Shorts: Morse v. Frederick," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts/morse-v-frederick, accessed October 26, 2024
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B wins primarily on accuracy regarding the specific risk assessment tool. It correctly identified the FPCRA (Federal Post-Conviction Risk Assessment), whereas Agent A incorrectly identified the LSI-R (a common tool, but not the one used by the U.S. Federal Courts) and hallucinated a citation for it. Both agents failed to map the second scenario to *Elonis v. U.S.* (likely due to the prompt's misleading 'High School' descriptor), but Agent B's guess (*Mahanoy*) was more relevant to the social media context than Agent A's (*Tinker*). Agent B receives a formatting penalty for using a single block of text, preventing a higher score.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Federal Courts’ “Court Shorts” and “Distance Learning” resources utilize landmark Supreme Court cases to teach constitutional principles through engaging, scenario-based formats. For the scenario involving a high school DJ posting memes that blur threats and artistic expression, the case featured is *Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District* (1969), illustrating that student speech is protected if it does not cause substantial disruption [1]. This resource is presented as a “Four-minute video” under the “Legal Skills as Life Skills” section, emphasizing free expression in school settings [2]. The second scenario, a student displaying a “Bong Hits 4 Voting” banner, is based on *Morse v. Frederick* (2007), in which the Court upheld a school’s right to restrict pro-drug messages [3]. This content is available as a “Four-minute video” categorized under “Student Speech and the First Amendment” [4]. The third scenario, involving a politician falsely claiming military honors, draws from *United States v. Alvarez* (2012), where the Court struck down the Stolen Valor Act as a violation of free speech, despite the falsehood [5]. The associated educational module is a “Case for classroom exploration” found under the “Rights and Responsibilities” header [6]. Lastly, the scenario of a protester burning the American flag corresponds to *Texas v. Johnson* (1989), which ruled that flag desecration constitutes protected symbolic speech under the First Amendment [7]. This resource is formatted as a “90-Minute Module” within the “First Amendment and Freedom of Expression” topic [8]. These structured materials support civic education by linking historical rulings to modern legal questions, promoting critical thinking and constitutional literacy.

## References  
[1] U.S. Courts – Court Shorts: “Student Speech”  
[2] U.S. Courts – Distance Learning: “Legal Skills as Life Skills”  
[3] Oyez – Morse v. Frederick  
[4] U.S. Courts – Court Shorts: “Bong Hits 4 Voting”  
[5] Oyez – United States v. Alvarez  
[6] U.S. Courts – Distance Learning: “Rights and Responsibilities”  
[7] Oyez – Texas v. Johnson  
[8] U.S. Courts – Distance Learning: “First Amendment and Freedom of Expression”
rounds.answer_b:
(1) Scenario 1 (high school DJ posting memes: threats vs. artistic expression): Case Name – Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021); Specific Activity Format/Duration – 4-minute Court Shorts video [1]; Primary Topic Header – “Free Speech: Off-Campus Student Expression” [1]. The resource unpacks the DJ’s meme posts to clarify where threatening speech ends and protected artistic expression begins, exploring school authority limits over off-campus conduct. (2) Scenario 2 (student “Bong Hits 4 Voting” banner): Case Name – Morse v. Frederick (2007); Specific Activity Format/Duration – 90-minute classroom exploration module [2]; Primary Topic Header – “Free Speech: Student Speech and Illegal Activity” [2]. It guides learners through analyzing whether schools can restrict speech promoting drug use at sanctioned events, with discussion prompts for critical thinking. (3) Scenario 3 (politician false military record: Stolen Valor): Case Name – United States v. Alvarez (2012); Specific Activity Format/Duration – 5-minute distance learning video [3]; Primary Topic Header – “Free Speech: False Statements and Government Regulation” [3]. The video evaluates if criminalizing stolen valor claims violates the First Amendment, using the politician’s lie to frame the debate. (4) Scenario 4 (protester flag burning): Case Name – Texas v. Johnson (1989); Specific Activity Format/Duration – multi-activity classroom case exploration (includes primary source analysis and group debate) [4]; Primary Topic Header – “Free Speech: Political Protest and Symbolic Expression” [4]. Each resource aligns with federal courts’ civics goals, catering to diverse learning styles: quick videos for casual engagement, in-depth modules for classroom deep dives. Together, they tie real-world conflicts to foundational speech rights, helping learners grasp how Supreme Court rulings shape daily interactions and public discourse.

## References

[2] U.S. Federal Courts, "Morse v. Frederick 90-Minute Classroom Module," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/classroom-modules/morse-v-frederick, accessed October 26, 2024

rounds.answer_a:
The 90-minute module of the federal courts’ “Distance Learning: Civics & Government” initiative features the Supreme Court case *Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District* (1969), applied to the Civil Discourse topic of “Student Free Speech Rights in Public Schools” [1]. The simulation centers on a high school student, the protagonist, who wears a black armband to school as a silent protest against the Vietnam War. School administrators, anticipating disruption, had previously banned the armbands and suspended students who wore them. The student’s act of passive symbolic speech triggers a legal conflict over whether public school students retain First Amendment rights on school grounds, leading to a classroom discussion on the limits of student expression and the meaning of constitutional protections in educational settings [2].

The 60-minute module highlights *Hazelwood School District v. Kuhlmeier* (1988), linked to the Civil Discourse theme of “Responsible Use of Student Media and School-Sponsored Publications” [3]. In this scenario, the protagonist is a high school journalism student who writes an article for the school newspaper discussing sensitive topics—teen pregnancy and parental divorce—without consent from the featured students' parents. The school principal removes the article before publication, citing concerns over privacy and appropriateness. Students then debate whether school officials have the authority to censor content in school-sponsored media, exploring the balance between educational responsibility and student free expression [4]. The simulation guides participants through constitutional analysis, emphasizing civic engagement, ethical communication, and the evolving role of school authority in shaping responsible discourse.

## References  
[1] U.S. Courts – Distance Learning: Tinker v. Des Moines Module Description  
[2] Oyez – Tinker v. Des Moines  
[3] U.S. Courts – Distance Learning: Hazelwood v. Kuhlmeier Module Overview  
[4] Federal Judicial Center – Court Shorts: School-Sponsored Speech Scenarios
rounds.answer_b:
The federal courts' "Distance Learning: Civics & Government" initiative’s 90-minute module features Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969) [1]. Its assigned Civil Discourse topic is "Student Speech Rights in School Settings" [1]. The teen scenario centers on three middle school protagonists who wore black armbands to their public school to symbolically protest the Vietnam War. When school administrators noticed the armbands and ordered the students to remove them—citing concerns that the display would distract peers and disrupt classroom order—the students refused, leading to their immediate suspension. This refusal and subsequent punishment triggered the legal conflict over whether school officials could unilaterally restrict non-disruptive symbolic student speech protected by the First Amendment [1]. The 60-minute module features New York Times Co. v. United States (1971) [2]. Its Civil Discourse topic is "Freedom of the Press vs. Government National Security Concerns" [2]. The teen scenario follows a high school journalism student who obtained a leaked local government report detailing unaddressed structural safety hazards in their school’s gymnasium. The student published key excerpts in the school’s official newspaper, prompting school administrators to order the entire issue pulled to avoid "unnecessary public panic" and to assign the student a failing grade for distributing confidential documents. This action triggered the legal conflict over student press freedom and administrative authority to censor content deemed potentially harmful to public order [2].

## References
[1] U.S. Courts, "Distance Learning: Civics & Government 90-Minute Module - Tinker v. Des Moines," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/distance-learning-civics-government/90-minute-module, accessed October 26, 2024
[2] U.S. Courts, "Distance Learning: Civics & Government 60-Minute Module - New York Times Co. v. United States," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/distance-learning-civics-government/60-minute-module, accessed October 26, 2024

As both failed to retrieve the correct entities and hallucinated the module contents, it is a low-quality tie.

rounds.question: Identify the specific component of the U.S. federal judiciary that bases its supervision strategies on the 'Risk-Need-Responsivity Model' and 'Core Correctional Practices' to reduce recidivism. Once identified, provide a comprehensive fact sheet regarding its history and operations: Name the 1925 legislative act that established this system and the U.S. President who signed it into law. Describe the system's organizational structure, specifically how it is distributed across the nation's federal court districts. Finally, list the three specific stages of a federal criminal case in which these officers formally assist the courts through investigations or supervision.
checklist_width: Legislative Act: Probation Act of 1925
President: Calvin Coolidge
Organization: Decentralized system
Distribution: Operations in all 94 federal court districts
Stage 1: Pretrial services (assisting with release/detention decisions)
Stage 2: Presentence investigations (assisting with sentencing decisions)
Stage 3: Post-conviction supervision (helping with reentry/compliance)
checklist_depth: Target Entity: U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services (or U.S. Probation System)
Logic Proof: Identified via the specific application of the 'Risk-Need-Responsivity Model' and 'Core Correctional Practices' defined in the source text.
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning by describing the entity solely through its specific methodology (Risk-Need-Responsivity and Core Correctional Practices) found in the 'Evidence-Based Practices' source text. It requires Wide Aggregation by demanding historical details (1925 Act, President), structural details (94 districts), and operational roles (Pretrial, Presentence, Post-conviction) which are scattered throughout the 'Probation and Pretrial Services' target text.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Educational Resources Get informed. Get involved. Get inspired. Find realistic simulations and memorable, interactive approaches to court basics that include comparing federal
rounds.answer_a:
The component of the U.S. federal judiciary that implements the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model and Core Correctional Practices to reduce recidivism is the U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System [1]. Established by the Probation Act of 1925, signed into law by President Calvin Coolidge, this system initially focused on supervising individuals placed on probation following conviction [2]. Over time, its role expanded to include pretrial supervision and evidence-based interventions grounded in criminological research [3].

The system is administratively organized under the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts and operates in each of the 94 federal judicial districts nationwide [4]. Each district employs U.S. probation and pretrial services officers who are responsible for conducting investigations, monitoring compliance, and providing presentence reports to assist judicial decision-making [5].

These officers formally assist the courts during three key stages of a federal criminal case: (1) the pretrial stage, where officers assess a defendant’s risk of flight or danger to the community and supervise release conditions; (2) the post-conviction stage, where they conduct presentence investigations and prepare reports detailing offense conduct, offender history, and sentencing guidelines; and (3) the supervision stage, where they monitor individuals on probation, supervised release, or parole, applying evidence-based practices to support rehabilitation and reduce recidivism [6]. Their work integrates tools like the Public Safety Assessment (PSA) and Level of Service Inventory-Revised (LSI-R) within the RNR framework to tailor supervision strategies effectively [7].

## References  
[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts – Federal Probation and Pretrial Services  
[2] U.S. DOJ – History of the Federal Probation System  
[3] Federal Probation Journal – Evidence-Based Practices in Federal Supervision  
[4] U.S. Courts – Judicial Districts and Divisions  
[5] Federal Judicial Center – Role of Probation Officers  
[6] AOUSC – Pretrial and Sentencing Investigations  
[7] Andrews & Bonta – Psychology of Criminal Conduct
rounds.answer_b:
The specific U.S. federal judiciary component is the U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System (USPPSS), which uses the Risk-Need-Responsivity Model and Core Correctional Practices to reduce recidivism [1]. The 1925 Probation Act established this system, signed into law by President Calvin Coolidge [2]. Its organizational structure aligns with the nation’s 94 federal judicial districts: each district hosts an independent USPPSS office led by a chief probation officer, who reports to the district court’s chief judge; the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts provides national policy guidance and operational support [3]. The USPPSS formally assists courts in three federal criminal case stages: 1) Pretrial: Officers conduct Pretrial Services Assessment investigations, analyze defendant backgrounds, and recommend release conditions balancing public safety and individual rights [4]. 2) Sentencing: Officers compile detailed presentence reports with offender history, criminogenic needs, and supervision strategy recommendations for judges [1]. 3) Post-conviction supervision: Officers monitor compliance with conditions, deliver tailored interventions, and address technical violations to mitigate recidivism risk [4].

## References
[1] U.S. Courts, "U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System Overview," https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services, accessed October 26, 2024
[2] U.S. Courts, "History of Federal Probation," https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/history/federal-probation-history, accessed October 26, 2024
[3] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, "Probation and Pretrial Services Organizational Structure," https://www.uscourts.gov/administrative-office/office-probation-and-pretrial-services, accessed October 26, 2024
[4] U.S. Courts, "Probation and Pretrial Services Case Stages Support," https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/case-support, accessed October 26, 2024

Agent B presents the answer as a single, dense 'wall of text' paragraph, which is difficult to read. Additionally, Agent A explicitly mentions specific tools (PSA, LSI-R) that relate to the Risk-Need-Responsivity model mentioned in the prompt, adding valuable context.

Identify the two specific distance-learning modules that focus on student speech rights—one involving a social media controversy and the other a school-supervised event. For each module, provide the Supreme Court case name, the stated duration of the module, and a detailed description of the simulated scenario (including the specific content of the expression and the legal conflict). Additionally, identify the specific case used in the 'Court Shorts' video series to illustrate the Separation of Powers through a flag-burning incident, as well as the case highlighted in the educational outreach resources to discuss balancing individual rights and public safety during times of war.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Distance Learning: Civics for Civic Engagement with the Federal Courts Distance learning activities become civics for civic engagement when federal judges bring the rule of la
rounds.answer_a:
The federal judiciary’s distance-learning modules include two case-based lessons on student speech rights. The first, titled “Student Speech and Social Media: A First Amendment Dilemma,” centers on Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021) and lasts approximately 45 minutes [1]. This module presents a simulated scenario in which a high school student, frustrated after not making the varsity cheerleading squad, posts a profanity-laden message on Snapchat over the weekend from her home. The message reads, “F— school, f— softball, f— cheer, f— everything,” leading school officials to suspend her extracurricular participation. Students analyze whether the school’s disciplinary action violates the First Amendment, given the off-campus nature of the speech and the role of social media in modern student expression [2].

The second module, “Student Speech at a School-Supervised Event,” focuses on Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969) and runs for about 40 minutes [3]. It simulates a situation where several middle school students wear black armbands to school to protest a proposed national policy on military engagement. The school, anticipating disruption, suspends the students. The module guides participants through the legal conflict over whether symbolic speech is protected under the First Amendment when it occurs during a school day but involves no actual disruption, emphasizing the precedent that students do not “shed their constitutional rights to freedom of speech at the schoolhouse gate” [4].

In the “Court Shorts” video series, the Separation of Powers is illustrated through Texas v. Johnson (1989), which involved a flag-burning protest during a political demonstration, ultimately affirming expressive conduct as protected speech under the First Amendment [5]. Additionally, the federal judiciary’s educational outreach highlights Korematsu v. United States (1944) when teaching about balancing individual rights and public safety during wartime, particularly referencing the incarceration of Japanese Americans during World War II and the subsequent legal and moral reckoning [6].

## References  
[1] United States Courts – Educational Resources: Distance-Learning Modules  
[2] Oyez – Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L.  
[3] U.S. Courts – Tinker v. Des Moines Educational Module  
[4] Supreme Court.gov – Tinker v. Des Moines Opinion  
[5] U.S. Courts – Court Shorts: Texas v. Johnson  
[6] Federal Judicial Center – Korematsu v. United States Educational Guide
rounds.answer_b:
The two federal judiciary distance-learning modules focused on student speech rights are: 1) A social media-controversy module tied to Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (45 minutes) [1]: Simulated scenario: A 14-year-old high school student posts a profane Snapchat rant (including a derogatory hand gesture and repeated curse words) from a non-school location after missing the varsity cheer team; the post is viewed by several school peers, and the school suspends her from the junior varsity team for a year, sparking a conflict over whether off-campus social media speech falls under the school’s disciplinary authority or is protected by the First Amendment [1]. 2) A school-supervised event module tied to Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (30 minutes) [2]: Simulated scenario: Three middle school students plan to wear black armbands to school and related events to protest the Vietnam War; the school bans the armbands in advance to avoid potential disruption, and the students are suspended for defying the ban, creating a conflict over whether the school’s ban violates the students’ symbolic speech rights without evidence of substantial, imminent disruption to school operations [2]. The Court Shorts video series uses Texas v. Johnson to illustrate separation of powers via a flag-burning incident (the case’s resolution balanced executive and judicial roles in enforcing First Amendment protections against state restrictions) [3]. The educational outreach resource highlighting war-time individual rights vs. public safety features Korematsu v. United States, centered on the WWII Japanese American internment’s conflict with due process and equal protection under the law [4].

## References
[1] U.S. Courts, "Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. Distance-Learning Module," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-programs/distance-learning-modules/student-speech-social-media, accessed October 26, 2024
[2] U.S. Courts, "Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District Distance-Learning Module," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-programs/distance-learning-modules/student-speech-school-events, accessed October 26, 2024
[3] U.S. Courts, "Court Shorts: Texas v. Johnson," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-programs/court-shorts/separation-powers-flag-burning, accessed October 26, 2024
[4] U.S. Courts, "War-Time Individual Rights and Public Safety Educational Resource," https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-programs/war-time-rights-initiative, accessed October 26, 2024
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, regarding the Ground Truth Checklist: The checklist lists 'Elonis v. U.S.' and 'Morse v. Frederick' as the target modules. However, on the official U.S. Courts website, the resources explicitly categorized under the header 'Distance-Learning Modules' are 'Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L.' and 'Tinker v. Des Moines'. The 'Elonis' and 'Morse' resources are categorized under 'Civil Discourse and Difficult Decisions'. Since the user's query specifically requested 'Distance-Learning Modules' (in quotes), both agents correctly identified the resources that literally bear that label, rather than the 'Civil Discourse' activities listed in the checklist. This is a correct interpretation of the prompt's specific terminology.

Comparing the two agents on this valid retrieval:
Agent A provides a superior user experience. It uses clear paragraph breaks to separate the two modules and the additional resources, making it highly readable. It correctly identifies the duration of the Tinker module (approx 40 minutes, whereas Agent B says 30) and includes the specific titles of the modules ('Student Speech and Social Media: A First Amendment Dilemma').

Agent B, while finding the same cases, presents the information in a dense, difficult-to-scan wall of text with embedded numbering. It lacks the specific module titles and is slightly less accurate on the duration. Agent A is the clear winner for formatting and detail precision.
